name: How flat does my subfloor need to be before new flooring is installed, and how do I fix low spots?
headline: How flat does my subfloor need to be before new flooring is installed, and how do I fix low spots?

mainEntity.name: How flat does my subfloor need to be before new flooring is installed, and how do I fix low spots?
mainEntity.text: How flat does my subfloor need to be before new flooring is installed, and how do I fix low spots?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most flooring manufacturers want the subfloor to be very flat before installation, usually within about 3/16 inch over 10 feet for plank products like laminate or vinyl. To check this, lay a long straightedge, level, or a straight 8–10 foot board on the floor and look for gaps or rocking. Mark high and low spots with a pencil. High areas can often be sanded or ground down. Low areas are usually filled with a cement-based or patching compound made for floors. Follow the product’s directions for mixing and drying times. Flat is not the same as perfectly level. A floor can be a little out of level and still accept new flooring, as long as there are no big dips or humps. What you are trying to avoid is movement in the new floor when you walk across it. If problems are severe, a contractor may recommend more involved repairs. Common mistakes include skipping this step, eyeballing the floor instead of measuring, or using the wrong patching material. In coastal areas with older homes, you may find uneven transitions between additions, or floors that settled over time. These almost always need some preparation. If you are hiring an installer, ask them exactly how they will check and correct the subfloor. A good pro should be able to explain their process and show you areas that need work before your new flooring goes down.
